question.name: Multiple selection problem.
question.text:
1.1 Multiple selection problem.

Which one of these is true?

<1>
Q1 Comments

View/make comments; ask/answer questions. Give feedback on the questions to help us improve. Comments are anonymous.

question.name: Multipart hybrid exercise
question.text:
1.2 Multipart hybrid exercise

If a=$a m/s and b=$b m/s, what is c?

  • (a)

  • (b)

<1>

If v=$c is the velocity of a car and x0 is the initial position, give an equation for the position of the car as a function of time t

<2>

Help

Equation Help

An instructor-led video working through the problem, auto-pausing when it's your turn to work. Good if you don't know where to start, or are stuck half-way through.
Check your own answer before revealing the answer. Revealing the answer takes away the chance to get it right yourself.
Only view worked solutions after making your best independent attempt. Seeing solutions ruins the learning opportunity. If you are stuck half-way, try (hard!) to only reveal necessary steps before continuing independently.

How to input expressions

The equation questions use Maple syntax to evaluate expressions. As with Python, basic operators use the symbols  + - * / ^ and parentheses can be used to structure your expressions. Some common functions can also be used such as sqrt(),  sin() and arcsin(). To get the natural exponential, use exp() as e is not intepreted as Euler's number.

For Greek letters, type the full name of the letter. For example mu*U  will render \( \mu U \) (there is no need to use a back slash).

Click the to preview your expressions.

For example, writing exp(i*pi) - 1 will produce:

\( \text{e}^{i \pi} - 1 \)

Q2 Comments

View/make comments; ask/answer questions. Give feedback on the questions to help us improve. Comments are anonymous.

question.name: Numerical-20260408_220141
question.text:
1.3 Numerical-20260408_220141

What is the gravitational acceleration on Earth in m/s^2?

<1>
Q3 Comments

View/make comments; ask/answer questions. Give feedback on the questions to help us improve. Comments are anonymous.

question.name: Multiple Choice-20260408_220141
question.text:
1.4 Multiple Choice-20260408_220141

What is Newton's Third Law?

<1>
Q4 Comments

View/make comments; ask/answer questions. Give feedback on the questions to help us improve. Comments are anonymous.

choices.choice: An object at rest stays at rest unless acted on by a net force.
If object A exerts a force on object B, then B exerts an equal and opposite force on A.

question.name: Text Entry-20260408_220141
question.text:
1.5 Text Entry-20260408_220141

Define a fluid.

<1>
Q5 Comments

View/make comments; ask/answer questions. Give feedback on the questions to help us improve. Comments are anonymous.

answers.answer: A fluid is a substance that deforms continuously under shear stress.

question.name: Symbolic
question.text:
1.6 Symbolic

Give the equation for the area of a Circle in terms of its diameter D?

<1>

Help

Equation Help

An instructor-led video working through the problem, auto-pausing when it's your turn to work. Good if you don't know where to start, or are stuck half-way through.
Check your own answer before revealing the answer. Revealing the answer takes away the chance to get it right yourself.
Only view worked solutions after making your best independent attempt. Seeing solutions ruins the learning opportunity. If you are stuck half-way, try (hard!) to only reveal necessary steps before continuing independently.

How to input expressions

The equation questions use Maple syntax to evaluate expressions. As with Python, basic operators use the symbols  + - * / ^ and parentheses can be used to structure your expressions. Some common functions can also be used such as sqrt(),  sin() and arcsin(). To get the natural exponential, use exp() as e is not intepreted as Euler's number.

For Greek letters, type the full name of the letter. For example mu*U  will render \( \mu U \) (there is no need to use a back slash).

Click the to preview your expressions.

For example, writing exp(i*pi) - 1 will produce:

\( \text{e}^{i \pi} - 1 \)

Q6 Comments
